Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'm 27, I've been around the block with education, and I just couldn't find the energy to be structured about it. I'd usually wait until I felt like I had to do something, then cram in some reading or writing. Most weeks I'd put in maybe 13 hours, but it was never at set times or anything planned out. I preferred reading and writing things down over watching videos, so I'd focus on that when I did study.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and whatever else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then suddenly realize something was due and scramble. I never really established a consistent weekly schedule. The stress of being behind would hit me, and then I'd try to catch up, but it wasn't sustainable. I kept telling myself I'd get more organized, but it never quite happened.
Q3: Usually it was panic, if I'm being honest. I'd get an email notification about a deadline or realize I hadn't checked in for a while and feel that scared, anxious feeling creeping up. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what I was missing, which often made me feel worse. Occasionally I'd go in to check discussion posts or see if there was feedback on something. But it wasn't a habit—it was more reactive than anything.
Q4: Not really, no. I tried a few times to make it a daily thing, especially after doing some mindfulness exercises they recommended. For maybe a week I'd log in regularly, but then life would get in the way or I'd feel overwhelmed and just... stop. The LMS never became natural for me. It always felt like a chore I was avoiding rather than something integrated into my day.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me because that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs or articles and actually process them at my own pace. Discussion posts were occasionally helpful when other students explained things differently than the instructor. I appreciated when there were clear, written summaries of what we needed to know. Videos weren't really my thing—I'd rather read the same content.
Q6: I ignored most of the videos, honestly. I'd start watching them but zone out or get distracted. The peer interaction stuff felt forced sometimes, so I'd do the minimum there. Optional readings or supplementary materials almost never got touched—I was barely keeping up with the required stuff. If something wasn't going to be graded or wasn't immediately necessary, it went to the bottom of my list and stayed there.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see the immediate payoff, I'd engage with it. Clear instructions and relevance to grades were the main factors. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't obviously connected to my final grade just frustrated me. At 27, I don't have patience for activities that seem like filler—I need to see the point.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. Required ones at least had the threat of consequences, so I'd force myself through them. With optional stuff, my brain would immediately calculate whether it was worth the effort, and the answer was usually no. I know that's not the best attitude, but when you're stressed and barely keeping up, optional feels like a luxury you can't afford. Required work got done—eventually. Optional work didn't.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download the readings and work through them on my tablet without being connected. I had notebooks where I'd write out key concepts and summaries—that's how information sticks for me. Sometimes I'd use external websites to clarify things the course materials didn't explain well. The LMS was more like a starting point; the real work happened elsewhere.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was genuinely studying but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd print out readings and annotate them by hand for hours. I'd rewrite my notes multiple times to memorize information. Sometimes I'd be working through problems on paper or discussing concepts with a friend over coffee. None of that shows up in any log. The system only sees clicks, not comprehension.
Q11: Absolutely. I'd access something once, download it, and then never go back to the LMS for that material. I'd study from the downloaded file for days or weeks. It was more convenient and I didn't need internet access. The LMS would show one brief login, but I might have spent ten hours with that material offline. It's just how I prefer to work—less distracting than being online.
Q12: There were definitely hard periods, usually when multiple deadlines piled up or when I was feeling particularly anxious about falling behind. I'd get scared looking at how much I hadn't done, and that fear would paralyze me instead of motivating me. Sometimes personal stuff would drain my energy too. During those times, even logging in felt overwhelming because I knew I'd see all the things I was failing at.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. The workload felt heavy, especially managing 18 courses worth of material. I'd get confused about priorities and what actually mattered most. Fatigue was real—I'm not 20 anymore, and late-night cramming hits different at 27. Work and other responsibilities competed for my attention. The LMS often lost that competition, even when I knew I should be engaging more.
Q14: Oh, definitely. I'd log in and click through things without really absorbing anything. My eyes would scan the page but my brain was elsewhere, worrying about other things or just exhausted. Sometimes I'd watch a video at 2x speed just to mark it complete, not actually learning anything. There were times I'd open discussion threads and just skim without really engaging with the ideas. Presence doesn't equal engagement.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even important ones. The fear of doing them poorly made me avoid starting them at all. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more prepared, but tomorrow would come with the same anxiety. Sometimes the task felt so big I didn't know where to start, so I just... didn't. My assignment completion rate shows that—42% is pretty damning. I knew it was self-sabotage but couldn't stop.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me use the LMS consistently. Without them, I'd drift for weeks. As a deadline approached, my activity would spike dramatically—panic-driven productivity. I'd cram everything into the last possible window. It wasn't healthy or effective, but it was my pattern. The structure of deadlines was both my enemy and my only real motivator.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback might motivate me briefly, but bad feedback would send me into avoidance mode. I'd feel scared to check my grades or comments because I didn't want confirmation that I was failing. When I did get constructive feedback, I'd try to apply it, but the emotional weight of criticism often outweighed its usefulness. I probably would've engaged more if feedback felt less like judgment.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it didn't really push me to engage consistently. Having everything available at once meant I could procrastinate indefinitely. Weekly modules might have helped me stay on track better. The flexibility that's supposed to be a benefit actually enabled my worst habits. I needed more structure than I got, but I also probably would've resented it if it was there.
Q19: The read/write materials were good for my learning style, so that helped. But the sheer volume of content across all my courses made it hard to prioritize. The discussion requirements felt arbitrary sometimes, which made me less motivated to participate meaningfully. Clearer connections between activities and assessments would have helped me engage more. The mindfulness intervention they offered was actually useful for my anxiety, though it didn't fix my engagement issues.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually producing work. They might be scared of starting assignments, so they just browse and prepare endlessly. Or they could be confused about what's expected and clicking around trying to figure it out. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes being online is just another form of procrastination—I've done that myself.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My exam score was decent—73—and I got a Merit despite low LMS engagement. I did a lot of learning offline through reading and writing notes. Some people just don't need to be constantly logged in to learn effectively. The LMS measures presence, not learning. If you're efficient with downloaded materials or have good study strategies outside the platform, you can perform without high visible activity.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, how long they're there, what they click on. They can identify someone who's completely absent versus somewhat present. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone's actually learning or just clicking through. They miss offline studying, emotional state, comprehension, and real understanding. The numbers tell a partial story at best.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious about the content and thinking about it even outside of study time. For me, real engagement is rare—it's when I'm not just scared of failing but actually interested in learning. It's mental presence, not just physical or digital presence. Most of my course experience wasn't true engagement by that definition.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared and anxious I felt throughout this course. It wouldn't capture the internal struggle between wanting to succeed and feeling paralyzed by fear of failure. It can't see the hours I spent with printed materials, genuinely trying to learn. It doesn't know about the mindfulness exercises I did trying to manage my stress. My emotional journey and offline efforts are completely invisible to any log system.
